title: Design of Low cost Multi-purpose Tapered Slot antenna Arrays for autonomous vehicles
description: Tapered Slot antenna Arrays are efficient for autonomous vehicles from the point of view of ultra wide band performance, cost effectiveness, compactness, high Directivity features. These are planer and Autonomous vehicles use antipodal configurations to influence cross polarization and bandwidth. The study will be primarily based on simulation platform performance analysis like Matlab, CST and later testing will be done on fabricated antennas under realistic conditions as much as possible. Artificial Intelligence simulation study will be done on open source data for possible use in driverless vehicles. Novel Methodology/Algorithm is to be developed with special attention to precision and Optimization.
keywords: Tapered Slot antenna; Autonomous vehicles; cross polarization; Optimization; Antipodal Configuration

title: Machine Learning Based Antenna Design for Gain Enhancement and Wireless Power Transmission
description: This UG thesis will utilize machine learning to optimize antenna designs, specifically targeting gain enhancement and wireless power transmission. By replacing slow, traditional electromagnetic simulations with predictive AI models, this approach drastically accelerates the development of highly efficient, high-gain antennas essential for next-generation wireless power and communication systems.
keywords: Machine Learning, Antenna Design, Radiation Pattern

title: DFT-Based Structural and Optical Properties Analysis of BiFeO3 Nanoparticles as a Promising Candidate for Next-Generation Photovoltaic Applications
description: This project investigates the structural and optical properties of rhombohedral perovskite BiFeO3 using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the Generalized Gradient Approximation (GGA). The optimized structural model shows good agreement with experimental data, supporting the reliability of the simulation results. The study reveals favorable mechanical stability, ductile behavior, a suitable direct band gap, and strong visible-light absorption characteristics. These findings highlight the potential of BiFeO3 as a promising multifunctional material for next-generation photovoltaic and optoelectronic applications.
keywords: Density Functional Theory; BiFeO3 Nanoparticles; Structural Properties; Optical Properties; Photovoltaic Applications

title: Investigation of Structural and Optical Properties of Cobalt doped BiFeO3 Nanoparticles Using Density Functional Theory for Next-Generation Photovoltaic Applications
description: This study presents a Density Functional Theory (DFT)-based investigation of the structural and optical properties of cobalt-doped BiFeO3 nanoparticles for potential photovoltaic applications. The effect of cobalt incorporation on the crystal structure and optical response is systematically analyzed to evaluate its influence on material performance. Particular emphasis is placed on optical behavior related to light absorption and electronic transitions, which are critical for solar energy conversion. The findings provide insight into the suitability of Co-doped BiFeO3 as a promising multifunctional material for next-generation photovoltaic technologies.
keywords: Density Functional Theory (DFT); Cobalt-Doped BiFeO3; Structural Properties; Optical Properties; Photovoltaic Applications

title: Fast Charging Station for Electric Vehicle with Renewable Energy Integration
description: Fast Charging Station for Electric Vehicle with Renewable Energy Integration refers to an EV charging system that delivers high-power DC fast charging using electricity from renewable sources like solar or wind, reducing grid dependence. It combines renewable generation, battery storage to handle intermittency, power converters, and grid backup, coordinated by an energy management system. This setup lowers carbon emissions, eases grid strain, cuts operating costs, and boosts energy resilience, particularly in remote areas.
keywords: Electric Vehicle; Fast Charging; Renewable Energy Integration; Solar Photovoltaic; Battery Management System

title: Smart Grid Energy Management Using Machine Learning
description: Smart Grid Energy Management Using Machine Learning refers to applying ML algorithms to optimize electricity generation, distribution, and consumption within smart grids. It involves predicting energy demand, forecasting renewable generation (solar/wind), detecting faults, optimizing load balancing, and enabling real-time decision-making for efficient power flow. Techniques like neural networks, regression models, and reinforcement learning analyze data from smart meters and sensors to improve grid reliability, reduce energy waste, and integrate renewable sources effectively. This approach enhances demand response, predictive maintenance, and dynamic pricing strategies, making power systems more efficient, resilient, and sustainable while supporting the growing complexity of modern, decentralized energy networks.
keywords: Smart Grid; Machine Learning; Energy Management System; Load Forecasting; Demand Prediction

title: State-of-Charge and State-of-Health Estimation of Battery Using Machine Learning
description: State-of-Charge (SoC) and State-of-Health (SoH) Estimation of Battery Using Machine Learning involves using ML algorithms to accurately predict a battery's remaining charge level (SoC) and its overall degradation or aging condition (SoH). Techniques like neural networks, support vector machines, and deep learning analyze data such as voltage, current, temperature, and charge-discharge cycles to model complex, nonlinear battery behavior. This approach improves accuracy over traditional methods, enabling better battery management, extended lifespan, and enhanced safety—particularly crucial for electric vehicles, renewable energy storage, and portable electronics where reliable real-time battery monitoring is essential for performance and longevity.
keywords: Battery State-of-Charge Estimation; State-of-Health Estimation; Battery Management System; Machine Learning / Deep Learning

title: Graphene-Enhanced Multi-Resonant Terahertz Metasurface Sensor for Ultra-Sensitive Refractive Index Sensing and Analyte Characterization
description: The increasing demand for highly sensitive, compact sensing platforms has driven the development of terahertz metasurface sensors. This research aims to design a graphene-enhanced multi-resonant metasurface capable of detecting and characterizing analytes through refractive index variations. The incorporation of graphene is expected to improve electromagnetic field confinement and resonance quality, thereby enhancing sensing performance. The study will investigate resonance behavior, electric field localization, sensitivity, figure of merit (FOM), quality factor (Q-factor), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and detection limit. The proposed sensor may find applications in chemical, environmental, industrial, and biological sensing systems.
keywords: Metasurface; Terahertz; Graphene; Multi-Resonant Sensor; Refractive Index Sensing; Bio-sensor

title: Machine Learning-Assisted Reconfigurable Terahertz Metasurface Sensor Based on Phase-Change Materials for Intelligent Multi-Analyte Identification
description: Reconfigurable metasurfaces have emerged as promising candidates for next-generation adaptive sensing systems. This research proposes the design of a terahertz metasurface integrated with a phase-change material, such as GST or VO2, to dynamically control resonance characteristics. By exploiting the reversible phase transition of the active material, the sensor can operate in multiple sensing states without geometric modification. Machine learning algorithms will be employed to analyze spectral responses and automatically identify analytes based on extracted resonance features. The study aims to establish an intelligent sensing framework that combines tunable electromagnetic functionality with data-driven classification techniques.
keywords: Terahertz Metasurface; ML based Intelligent Reconfigurable Sensor; Phase-Change Materials; Multi-Analyte Detection; Resonance Tuning

title: Hybrid Quantum Machine Learning Assisted Surface Plasmon Resonance Prism Sensor for Ultra-High-Sensitivity Biomedical Detection
description: Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) sensors provide highly sensitive, real-time detection of biomolecular interactions through refractive index monitoring. As sensor datasets become increasingly complex, conventional machine-learning techniques may face computational limitations. Hybrid Quantum Machine Learning (HQML) combines quantum computing and classical learning algorithms to enhance predictive modeling and feature extraction. This thesis investigates the integration of HQML with SPR biosensors to improve detection accuracy, optimize sensor performance, and establish a foundation for future ultra-high-sensitivity biomedical sensing and precision diagnostic applications.
keywords: Surface Plasmon Resonance; Quantum Machine Learning; Biomedical Detection; Intelligent Biosensing; High-Sensitivity Biosensors

title: Development of Switched beam Smart Antenna for Sub-6GHz Application
description: Rapid advances in wireless communication technologies have intensified the demand for high gain, improved coverage, and efficient spectrum utilization antenna, particularly in the sub-6G frequency range due to its widespread adoption in 5G and IoT applications. The objective of this thesis work will be as follows: Design a high-gain antenna array for generating multiple directional beams covering different angular sectors operating in the Sub-6 GHz band using CST/ADS/HFSS. Develop an adaptive switching algorithm based on direction of arrival (DOA) and received signal strength (RSSI).
keywords: Antenna, Switching beam, RSSI, sub-6GHz, CST/ADS/HFSS

title: ML based adaptive PID controller for PWR reactor coolant system during emergency condition
description: Recent research in adaptive PID controller design has moved beyond fixed-gain and classical tuning methods toward intelligent, data-driven, and hybrid adaptive approaches. Modern adaptive PID controllers increasingly integrate machine learning (ML) techniques, such as reinforcement learning and neural networks, to enable real-time gain adjustment under nonlinearities, uncertainties, and varying operating conditions. These methods allow controllers to learn system behavior online, improving accuracy and robustness. Thus the objective of this research work will be development of efficient and accurate ML based adaptive PID controller for reactor coolant control system.
keywords: ML; Adaptive; PID controller; PWR reactor

title: ML based power transformer remaining life prediction for Bangladesh's Power System
description: Transformer Remaining Life Estimation (RLE) is the process of predicting how many years a power transformer can continue operating safely before its insulation system reaches end-of-life. Remaining life prediction enables utilities to identify aging transformers, optimize maintenance schedules, improve system reliability, reduce operational costs, and ensure uninterrupted power supply. In Bangladesh due to limited maintenance resources, the development of intelligent transformer remaining life estimation techniques can significantly enhance system reliability. Dissolved gas analysis (DGA) is one of the most widely used condition-monitoring techniques for transformer health assessment and RLE prediction. The objective of this work is to find the efficient ML model to estimate the RLE based on DGA and transformer operational data.
keywords: Power transformer, RLE, DGA, ML model

title: Explainable Deep Multi-Modal Fusion of Phonocardiogram (PCG) and Electrocardiogram (ECG) Signals for Robust Valvular Heart Disease Screening
description: While isolated ECG or PCG classification is common, clinical diagnosis relies on both electrical (ECG) and mechanical/acoustic (PCG) cardiac activity. A major bottleneck in deploying deep learning models in clinics is the "black box" problem—doctors do not trust a model unless they see why it made a decision. This project proposes a dual-branch neural network (such as a parallel 1D-CNN and Transformer architecture) that simultaneously processes time-synchronized ECG and PCG recordings. Students will focus on developing a cross-attention mechanism that allows the model to learn the mathematical correlations between specific acoustic anomalies (like heart murmurs) and electrical anomalies (like localized ST-segment modifications).
keywords: ECG, PCG, DNN, XAI, HVD

title: Unsupervised Adversarial Domain Adaptation for Cross-Dataset Surface EMG Gesture Recognition
description: A major flaw in current surface EMG (sEMG) research is that a model trained on Subject A rarely works on Subject B due to physical differences, sensor placement shifts, and skin impedance. Most papers bypass this by testing on the same dataset. To publish in a top-tier journal, you need to solve the domain generalization problem. This thesis will investigate Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) using Adversarial Neural Networks. The objective is to train a feature extractor on a well-labeled source dataset (e.g., NinaPro Database) while simultaneously utilizing an unlabelled target dataset from a completely different environment or group of subjects. By introducing a domain discriminator loss, the network is forced to learn features that are invariant to the specific subject or sensor placement, focusing purely on the underlying muscle activation patterns.
keywords: EMG, sEMG, UDA, ANN, Gesture Recognition

title: Self-Supervised Masked Autoencoders for Electroencephalogram (EEG) Artifact Denoising and Sleep Stage Classification
description: Biomedical signal processing suffers from a data labeling bottleneck: it requires hundreds of hours of expert clinician time to score sleep stages or label EEG microstates. Furthermore, traditional filtering often strips away vital high-frequency neural information along with the noise. This project introduces Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)—specifically leveraging Masked Autoencoders (MAE) adapted for 1D signals. Instead of relying on labels, the model is trained on vast amounts of unlabeled EEG raw data by intentionally masking out (hiding) random segments (e.g., 50-70%) of the continuous wave. The network must learn to mathematically reconstruct the missing signal patches based on the surrounding context. Through this reconstruction process, the model inherently learns the deep, fundamental syntax of neural oscillations and becomes exceptionally robust at distinguishing true brain waves from sudden artifacts (muscle twitches, electrode movement). Once pre-trained, students will fine-tune the model on a very small labeled dataset for sleep stage classification (like the Sleep-EDF database), demonstrating that self-supervised pre-training drastically outperforms standard supervised models when data is scarce.
keywords: EEG, MAE, Masked Autoencoders, SSL, signal processing

title: Physics-Informed Learning for Charging Station Attack Analytics
description: This undergraduate thesis on "Physics-Informed Learning for Charging Station Attack Analytics" merges cybersecurity, data science, and electrical engineering to protect Electric Vehicle Charging Stations (EVCS) from malicious cyber-physical threats like false data injection or malware. Because traditional machine learning models can be fooled by sophisticated hacks or output physically impossible anomalies, this research leverages Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML). By embedding fundamental physical laws—such as Kirchhoff's voltage laws, Ohm's law, and battery thermal dynamics—directly into the AI's learning algorithms, the system can cross-reference incoming data with real-world physical constraints. The core of the thesis focuses on developing analytics and detection frameworks that can accurately differentiate between benign grid fluctuations and actual stealth attacks. Ultimately, this approach allows for real-time anomaly detection and quantifies the downstream impacts of an attack, such as accelerated EV battery degradation, thermal runaway risks, or severe voltage instability across the localized power distribution grid.
keywords: Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML), Electric Vehicle Charging Stations (EVCS), Cyber-Physical Security, Anomaly Detection, Power Grid Instability

title: Machine Learning for Energy Efficient IoMT Systems
description: This undergraduate thesis on "Machine Learning for Energy Efficient and Secured IoMT Systems" explores the critical intersection of healthcare, cybersecurity, and green computing within the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT). As wearable sensors, implantable cardiac devices, and remote patient monitoring tools become ubiquitous, they face a dual challenge: operating on severely constrained battery life while processing highly sensitive, life-critical medical data that is constantly targeted by cyber threats. This research focuses on utilizing lightweight Machine Learning (ML) algorithms—such as edge-based neural networks, support vector machines, or federated learning—to optimize resource allocation and predict patient health anomalies without draining device power. To achieve energy efficiency, the thesis investigates techniques like adaptive sampling, intelligent duty-cycling, and low-power communication protocols, where ML dynamically adjusts data transmission based on the patient's physiological urgency. Simultaneously, the framework addresses robust security paradigms by deploying ML for real-time anomaly detection, zero-trust authentication, and mitigation of cyberattacks like eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle, or data tampering. By shifting intelligence to the network edge, this approach reduces the energy overhead of continuous cloud communication while ensuring patient data privacy under regulations like HIPAA. Ultimately, the thesis aims to design an optimized, self-defending IoMT ecosystem that guarantees continuous, secure, and prolonged operation of medical devices, thereby safeguarding both patient health and data integrity in modern digital healthcare infrastructure.
keywords: Internet of Medical Things (IoMT), Machine Learning (ML), Energy Efficiency, Cybersecurity, Edge Computing

title: Hybrid Machine Learning Models for HVAC Fault Detection
description: An undergraduate thesis on "Hybrid Machine Learning Models for HVAC Fault Detection" investigates the intersection of smart building technologies, energy efficiency, and artificial intelligence to optimize Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) systems. HVAC systems consume up to half of the total energy in commercial and residential buildings, and undetected faults—such as sensor drift, refrigerant leaks, or compressor failures—drastically escalate energy waste and operational costs. While purely data-driven machine learning models are excellent at recognizing complex data patterns, they often require massive datasets and struggle to generalize when unexpected operational shifts occur. Conversely, traditional physics-based models are highly accurate but computationally expensive and difficult to scale across different building architectures. To overcome these limitations, this research focuses on developing hybrid models that fuse the strengths of both approaches. By combining data-driven techniques (such as deep neural networks or random forests) with physical domain knowledge (such as thermodynamic principles and mass-energy balance equations), the hybrid framework achieves superior predictive accuracy with fewer training data requirements. The thesis explores how these models process multi-sensor time-series data to perform real-time automated fault detection and diagnostics (AFDD). Additionally, the study addresses the challenges of distinguishing between actual mechanical faults, sensor anomalies, and normal baseline shifts caused by changing weather or occupancy patterns. Ultimately, this research aims to provide a robust, scalable, and highly interpretable AI solution that minimizes building energy footprints, reduces maintenance downtime, and extends the lifespan of critical climate-control infrastructure.
keywords: Hybrid Machine Learning, HVAC Fault Detection and Diagnostics (FDD), Smart Building Energy Efficiency, Physics-Based Modeling, Time-Series Sensor Analytics

title: Design a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor Controller Module for Electric Vehicles
description: This thesis focuses on the design and development of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) controller module specifically tailored for electric vehicle (EV) propulsion systems. PMSM machines are widely used in EV applications due to their high efficiency, high torque density, compact size, and excellent dynamic performance. However, their operation requires a precise and robust control strategy to ensure optimal performance across varying speed and load conditions. The primary objective of the thesis is to design a controller module capable of regulating the torque, speed, and position of a PMSM under real-world EV operating scenarios. The control system typically employs advanced vector control techniques, such as Field-Oriented Control (FOC), to decouple the motor's torque and flux components, enabling independent and efficient control. The controller processes feedback signals from current sensors, position encoders, and voltage measurements to continuously adjust the inverter switching signals. A key component of the system is the power electronics interface, usually a three-phase voltage source inverter (VSI), which converts DC battery power into controlled AC supply for the motor. The thesis includes the design of the control algorithms, simulation of motor behavior, and implementation of pulse width modulation (PWM) strategies for efficient switching control. Emphasis is also placed on system stability, response time, energy efficiency, and regenerative braking capability. The work may be implemented using embedded hardware platforms such as FPGAs or microcontrollers (e.g., STM32, TI C2000), with validation through simulation tools like MATLAB/Simulink, Ansys, SolidWorks etc. Protection features such as overcurrent, overvoltage, and thermal monitoring are also integrated into the controller design. Overall, the thesis aims to deliver a high-performance, reliable, and efficient 10KW PMSM controller module that can enhance the drivability and energy efficiency of electric vehicles while maintaining system robustness and safety.
keywords: PMSM; EV; Motor Controller; Power Electronics; FPGAs

title: Hybrid Photovoltaic–Hydraulic Mechanical Battery System with Controlled Discharge Turbine for Renewable Power Supply Applications
description: This thesis focuses on the design and analysis of a hybrid photovoltaic–hydraulic mechanical battery system intended for renewable energy storage and controlled power generation. The system integrates solar photovoltaic (PV) panels with a pressurized water storage mechanism to enable energy capture during daylight hours and electricity generation during night-time or peak demand periods. During the charging phase, electrical energy produced by the PV array is used to drive a pump system that compresses water into high-pressure storage cylinders or reservoirs. This process converts intermittent solar energy into stored hydraulic potential energy. The design of the pumping system, pressure vessels, and energy conversion efficiency are key aspects of the study, with emphasis on minimizing losses during energy transfer and compression. In the discharge phase, the stored pressurized water is released in a controlled manner through a hydraulic turbine connected to an electric generator. The turbine converts hydraulic energy back into mechanical energy and subsequently into electrical energy. A key component of the system is the controlled discharge mechanism, which regulates flow rate and pressure to maintain stable turbine operation and consistent power output. The thesis includes the development of system models to analyze energy conversion efficiency, storage capacity, pressure dynamics, and turbine performance. Control strategies are incorporated to optimize charging and discharging cycles based on energy demand and storage levels. Simulation tools such as MATLAB/Simulink, Ansys, Solid Works or equivalent fluid dynamics modeling software may be used to evaluate system behavior under varying environmental and load conditions. The proposed hybrid system aims to provide a sustainable, scalable, and off-grid energy storage solution, particularly suitable for remote areas and microgrid applications. By combining solar energy harvesting with mechanical hydraulic storage, the system addresses intermittency issues of renewable energy and enhances overall energy reliability and utilization efficiency.
keywords: Hydraulic Energy Storage; Mechanical Battery; Renewable Energy; Hydro Power; Off-Grid Electricity

title: Hybrid Electric Vehicle Charging Station: Design, Modeling, and Performance Evaluation
description: The increasing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) is placing greater demand on existing power grids, creating a need for more charging infrastructure. A hybrid micro-grid-based EV charging station that combines solar and wind energy with the utility grid to provide a sustainable and cost-effective charging solution. The aims of this research are to enhance charging efficiency while reducing environmental impact and operational costs.
keywords: Electric vehicle, charging station, microgrid, solar energy, wind energy

title: Analysis of electrical parameters of Silicon on Insulator (SOI) devices by modeling
description: Silicon-on-insulator (SOI) technology provides notable benefits over traditional bulk silicon technology, particularly in the fabrication of integrated circuits (ICs), including complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) ICs. In this work, key parameters such as kink effect, threshold voltage, subthreshold slope, negative transconductance, on-current and leakage current of SOI will be determined.
keywords: Silicon on Insulator, Kink Effect, Negative transconductance, Subthreshold slope

title: Investigation of electrical characteristics of Partially-Depleted Silicon on Insulator (PD-SOI) devices by simulation
description: The device structure and operation of partially-depleted silicon on insulator (SOI) device will be investigated. Moreover, key parameters such as threshold voltage, drain induced barrier lowering, on-current and negative transconductance of PD-SOI will be determined.
keywords: Partially-Depleted, Silicon on Insulator, Drain induced barrier lowering, Negative transconductance

title: Study of electrical parameters of Fully-Depleted Silicon on Insulator (FD-SOI) devices by modeling
description: In Fully-Depleted Silicon on Insulator (FD-SOI) devices, the ultra-thin body silicon layer introduces sensitivity to channel thickness variations, directly impacting electrical properties. In this work, key parameters such as threshold voltage, drain induced barrier lowering, on-current, temperature sensitivity, and leakage current of FD-SOI will be determined.
keywords: Fully-Depleted, Silicon on Insulator, Drain induced barrier lowering, Temperature sensitivity, Threshold Voltage

title: Design and Performance Analysis of a Tamm Plasmon Polariton (TPP)-Based Photonic Crystal Biosensor
description: This project aims to design and investigate a Tamm Plasmon Polariton (TPP)-based biosensor by integrating a metal layer with a one-dimensional photonic crystal structure. The proposed sensor will exploit the strong electromagnetic field confinement at the metal–photonic crystal interface to achieve high sensitivity toward variations in the refractive index of biological analytes. Numerical modeling and simulation techniques, such as the Transfer Matrix Method (TMM), will be employed to analyze the optical response of the structure and optimize its sensing performance. This project will show how Tamm plasmon resonance can be utilized to enhance biosensing capabilities through improved sensitivity, narrow resonance linewidth, and high-quality factor. The effects of structural parameters, material selection, and analyte refractive index variations on sensor performance will be systematically investigated. The outcomes of this research are expected to contribute to the development of compact, label-free, and highly sensitive biosensors for biomedical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and chemical detection applications.
keywords: Tamm Plasmon Polariton (TPP); Biosensor; Label-Free Detection

title: Design, Performance Analysis, and Optimization of a Hybrid Plasmonic Biosensor for Highly Sensitive and Accurate Detection of Cancer Biomarkers toward Early Cancer Diagnosis
description: Early detection of cancer significantly improves treatment outcomes and patient survival. This thesis aims to design and optimize a hybrid plasmonic biosensor for the highly sensitive and accurate detection of cancer biomarkers. The study will investigate different material combinations and structural parameters to enhance the sensor's performance in terms of sensitivity, detection accuracy, quality factor (Q-factor), figure of merit (FOM), and detection limit. Numerical simulations will be carried out using suitable electromagnetic modeling techniques to evaluate the sensor's optical characteristics. The optimized biosensor is expected to provide rapid, label-free, and reliable detection of cancer biomarkers, making it a promising platform for early cancer diagnosis and future biomedical sensing applications.
keywords: Plasmonic, Biosensor, cancer diagnosis

title: Design, Performance Analysis, and Optimization of a Microwave Resonator-Based Biosensor for Highly Sensitive and Accurate Disease Detection
description: Early and accurate disease detection plays a vital role in improving clinical diagnosis and patient care. This thesis focuses on the design, performance analysis, and optimization of a microwave resonator-based biosensor for highly sensitive and accurate disease detection. The research will investigate different resonator geometries, substrate materials, and design parameters to enhance key performance metrics such as resonance frequency shift, quality factor (Q-factor), sensitivity, and detection accuracy. Electromagnetic simulations will be conducted to evaluate and optimize the sensor's performance under different sensing conditions. The proposed biosensor is expected to provide a rapid, cost-effective, and reliable sensing platform with potential applications in biomedical diagnostics and point-of-care disease detection.
keywords: Resonator, Biosensor, biomedical diagnostics

title: Computational Investigation of Ion-Sensitive Field-Effect Transistor Based Biosensors
description: Ion-Sensitive Field-Effect Transistors (ISFETs) are important biosensing devices capable of detecting changes in ion concentration through variations in their electrical characteristics. Due to their high sensitivity, fast response, compact size, and compatibility with integrated circuits, ISFET-based biosensors have attracted significant attention for applications in healthcare, environmental monitoring, and food safety. This thesis aims to investigate the performance of ISFET-based biosensors through computational modeling and simulation. The study will focus on understanding the device operation and analyzing the effects of key design parameters, such as semiconductor materials, dielectric layers, sensing membranes, and device dimensions, on sensor performance. Numerical simulations will be used to evaluate important characteristics including sensitivity, threshold voltage shift, and response behavior under varying ionic concentrations. The project will provide insights into the optimization of ISFET structures for enhanced sensing performance while offering students practical experience in device simulation and biosensor technology. The outcomes are expected to contribute to the development of efficient and reliable ISFET-based biosensors for future sensing applications.
keywords: ISFET; Biosensing; Simulation; Detection; Optimization

title: Photonic crystal fibre sensor for unhealthy food additive detection
description: Food security remains a paramount concern globally, as the presence of hazardous species or harmful additives poses a genuine threat to both food safety and public health. Certain food additives such as sorbitol, butyl acetate, and saccharin are the cause of health risks if they remain present in foods. To ensure the safety and compliance of food products, forming an assigned food additive sensing device capable of detecting a wide range of additives in diverse food items is required. This study centers on employing photonic crystal fiber for detection of these unsafe food additives. The structure of the PCF sensor allows for precise light control, leading to high sensitivity and selectivity. This enables the detection of specific contaminants like saccharin, sorbitol, and butyl acetate.
keywords: Photonic crystal fiber (PCF), Effective area (EA), Numerical aperture (NA), Relative sensitivity (RS), Refractive index (RI)

title: An Embedded AI-Based Autonomous Vehicle Control System for Emergency Intervention Using Driver Drowsiness Detection
description: The goal of this project is to create an autonomous vehicle control system that can intervene in a driver drowsy emergency and is embedded within the vehicle. Using an on-board camera and embedded computing platform, the system is continuously assessing the driver's facial features, eye movements, head pose and alertness in real time with the use of multiple artificial intelligence techniques. The system takes control of the vehicle once persistent drowsiness is detected, and switches the system to an emergency autonomous driving mode, safely bringing the vehicle to the roadside or a safe place before executing the parking manoeuvre automatically. At the same time, the system alerts pre-programmed emergency contacts to the vehicle's position and crisis to enable quick help. The project also tests the detection accuracy, response time, autonomous control performance and overall reliability of the proposed system, which could help to mitigate accidents related to driver fatigue and improve road safety.
keywords: Driver Drowsiness Detection, Autonomous Vehicle Control, Embedded Artificial Intelligence, Emergency Response System

title: Frequency Selective Surface-Assisted MIMO Antenna design
description: The rapid evolution of modern wireless communication systems has increased the demand for high-performance Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) antennas capable of supporting enhanced data throughput, spectral efficiency, and link reliability. Despite these advantages, mutual coupling and electromagnetic interaction among radiating elements remain significant challenges, often leading to degraded isolation, increased signal correlation, and reduced system performance. Consequently, the development of effective decoupling techniques has become an important area of research. This proposed study aims to investigate the integration of Frequency Selective Surface (FSS) structures with MIMO antenna systems to improve electromagnetic performance. The FSS, composed of periodically arranged resonant elements, can selectively manipulate electromagnetic waves and suppress undesired coupling mechanisms within a targeted frequency range. By appropriately designing the FSS geometry and its electromagnetic characteristics, improvements in isolation, Envelope Correlation Coefficient (ECC), diversity performance, and overall channel capacity are expected. A major challenge in FSS-assisted MIMO design is achieving coupling reduction without adversely affecting impedance matching, radiation characteristics, bandwidth, or antenna efficiency. Additionally, the resonant nature of conventional FSS structures often limits their operational bandwidth. To address these challenges, advanced unit-cell geometries, multi-layer configurations, and metamaterial-inspired designs will be explored to realize broadband and low-loss performance. Optimization techniques will also be employed to identify design parameters that provide an optimal trade-off between isolation enhancement and radiation performance. The outcomes of this research are expected to provide valuable design guidelines for next-generation wireless, radar, and sensing systems, where compact architectures and high electromagnetic performance are essential.
keywords: MIMO; antenna; FSS

title: An Edge-Native TinyML Engine for Non-Intrusive Load Scheduling and Prepaid Credit Management in Solar-Assisted Smart Homes with Explainable AI and Autonomous Override
description: The system measures real-time voltage and current at a single point of entry to compute instantaneous power and cumulative energy consumption, maintaining a virtual credit register against user-defined threshold limits and triggering immediate mobile notifications via lightweight MQTT/WebSocket protocols when credits are depleted.
keywords: Home Energy Management System (HEMS); TinyML; Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM); Explainable AI (XAI); Solar BESS

title: Transient stability enhancement via index-based FIDVR mitigation in renewable-dominant Multi-DG (hybrid) industrial microgrids with optimized reactive power support
description: Renewable-dominant multi-DG hybrid industrial microgrids are increasingly exposed to transient stability challenges due to fault-induced delayed voltage recovery (FIDVR), especially when large induction motor loads, intermittent renewable generation, and weak voltage support coexist. Following disturbance events, delayed restoration of terminal voltage can trigger motor stalling, reactive power depletion, and cascading distributed generator (DG) trips, threatening secure microgrid operation. To address this issue, this study proposes an index-based methodology for enhancing transient stability through optimized reactive power support. The proposed approach introduces a voltage-recovery-oriented index to identify the most vulnerable buses in the microgrid based on post-fault voltage behavior, recovery speed, and reactive power demand. These index values are then used to determine the optimal location and size of reactive power support devices, such as battery energy storage systems or other dynamic compensating units, to improve voltage restoration after fault clearance. The methodology is designed for renewable-dominant hybrid microgrids containing multiple DG sources, where varying power injections and load dynamics make conventional compensation strategies less effective. This framework provides utilities with a scalable, index-driven tool for resilient, renewable-rich industrial microgrids, offering undergraduate students a practical foundation for FIDVR mitigation research, reactive power optimization, and microgrid stability analysis under variable renewable penetration.
keywords: Microgrid resilience, FIDVR, Transient stability, Optimization, Reactive power compensation

title: Reliability-Constrained Multi-Objective Optimal Placement of Micro-Phasor Measurement Units for Complete Observability of Smart Distribution Networks
description: The research aims to determine the optimal placement of μPMUs in smart distribution networks while considering both observability and reliability requirements. A multi-objective optimization framework will be developed to minimize the number of μPMUs and associated costs while ensuring complete network observability under normal operating conditions and selected contingency scenarios. Metaheuristic optimization techniques will be employed and the proposed methodology will be validated by demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing grid observability, reliability, and resilience.
keywords: μPMU; Optimization; Metaheuristic algorithm; Smart grid; Observability

title: Coordinated Operation of Synchronous Generators and Grid-Forming Inverters for Voltage Stability Support
description: Maintaining voltage stability is a critical challenge for modern power grids, especially as traditional synchronous generators (SGs) are replaced by renewable energy sources interfaced through power electronics. The coordinated operation of SGs and grid-forming (GFM) inverters offers a powerful solution. Synchronous generators are the backbone of conventional grids, providing inherent voltage support through their excitation systems and a physical inertia that resists frequency changes. They excel at supplying large reactive power and maintaining a stiff voltage profile. However, their response is relatively slow and tied to electromechanical dynamics. Grid-forming inverters, conversely, are a new class of power electronics that emulate the behavior of a synchronous machine. Instead of just following the grid's voltage and frequency (like grid-following inverters), GFM inverters act as voltage sources, actively establishing and regulating the local grid's voltage and frequency. They offer an extremely fast and precise response, capable of injecting or absorbing reactive power within milliseconds to counter sudden voltage dips. Their coordination with SGs creates a synergistic effect. SGs provide the long-term, robust reactive power reserves and system inertia, ensuring stability during severe disturbances. GFM inverters complement this with their ultra-fast dynamic voltage support, acting as a "first line of defense" against transient voltage collapses. This coordinated strategy enhances the grid's overall resilience, reduces stress on conventional generators, and is essential for integrating high penetrations of renewables while maintaining a stable and secure power system.
keywords: Voltage stability; Coordinated operation; Synchronous generators (SGs); Grid-forming (GFM) inverters; Reactive power support

title: Design and Analysis of a Radiation-hardened SRAM Cell for Space Applications
description: A radiation-hardened SRAM circuit is a memory design made to work reliably in environments with radiation. It is used in places like satellites, spacecraft, and other systems where normal memory may be affected by energetic particles. These particles can disturb the stored data and cause bit flips, which makes the memory less dependable. To reduce this problem, the circuit is built with special methods that improve stability and protect the stored information. Some designs use extra transistors, while others change the cell structure or add correction methods. The main goal is to keep the memory working correctly even when the surrounding environment is harsh. Overall, radiation-hardened SRAM is an important part of dependable electronics for critical applications. It helps ensure that stored data remains safe and that systems continue to operate properly in difficult conditions.
keywords: SRAM, Radiation, Tolerance

title: Cross-Dataset Benchmarking of Classical, Ensemble, and Graph-Based Deep Learning Models for EEG Psychiatric Disorder Classification
description: This thesis presents a unified benchmarking framework evaluating how model complexity and EEG data representation jointly affect psychiatric disorder classification accuracy and generalizability. Across one or more EEG datasets—spanning tabular features and raw signals, resting-state and task-based paradigms—it compares three modeling tiers: linear baselines (Elastic Net), ensemble methods (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, Random Forest), and graph-based deep learning that encodes EEG channels as connected nodes. Each dataset follows a consistent protocol of subject-independent splitting, confound analysis, and class-imbalance handling for fair comparison. Interpretability is assessed via SHAP (classical/ensemble) and attention/node-importance (graph-based) methods. The framework is dataset-agnostic, allowing any psychiatric or neurological EEG dataset to be substituted without changing the methodology. The central question: does architectural complexity yield real gains once confounds are controlled, or are gains dataset-specific? The result is a reproducible benchmarking pipeline rather than a single model, publishable as a standalone methods contribution.
keywords: EEG classification, cross-dataset benchmarking, graph neural networks, ensemble learning, confound control

title: Investigation of Emerging Field-Effect Transistor Based Biosensors for Label-Free Biomolecule Detection: A TCAD Study
description: The increasing demand for rapid, accurate, and label-free detection of biological analytes has accelerated research into semiconductor-based biosensors for applications in healthcare diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and food safety. Among various sensing technologies, field-effect transistor (FET) based biosensors have emerged as promising candidates due to their high sensitivity, low power consumption, compact size, and compatibility with modern semiconductor fabrication processes. However, conventional biosensor architectures often encounter limitations related to sensitivity, selectivity, and scalability when detecting low-concentration biomolecules. This research aims to investigate and optimize advanced semiconductor biosensor structures using Silvaco TCAD simulation. The study will focus on the influence of device architecture, material engineering, and sensing region design on biosensor performance. Different sensing mechanisms, including dielectric modulation and biomolecule charge effects, will be analyzed to understand their impact on electrical characteristics such as drain current, threshold voltage, transconductance, subthreshold swing, and sensitivity. The proposed work will evaluate the detection of various biomolecules represented by different dielectric constants and charge densities within a nanocavity or sensing region integrated into the device structure. Furthermore, emerging device concepts such as junctionless transistors, tunnel field-effect transistors, negative-capacitance devices, and other advanced architectures may be explored to improve sensing performance and energy efficiency. Device optimization will be performed through systematic variation of structural and material parameters, including gate dielectric properties, cavity dimensions, channel characteristics, and gate engineering techniques. The outcomes of this work are expected to provide valuable insights into the design of next-generation semiconductor biosensors with enhanced sensitivity and reliability. The findings may contribute to the development of compact, low-power, and highly efficient biosensing platforms suitable for future biomedical and environmental sensing applications.
keywords: Biosensor; Field-Effect Transistor; Sensitivity; Dielectric Modulation

title: Design and Characteristic Analysis of Thin-Film Transistor (TFT) Based Integrated Circuits Using SPICE
description: Thin-Film Transistors (TFTs) represent a foundational technology for the realization of flexible, large-area, and low-cost macroelectronics, driving innovations in active-matrix displays, wearable sensors, smart packaging, and electronic skins. As the field transitions from isolated devices to complex integrated systems, a deep understanding of TFT-based circuit behavior is crucial. This research presents a systematic performance and characteristic analysis of fundamental TFT-based circuit topologies to evaluate their reliability and functionality in next-generation microelectronics applications. Utilizing industry-standard circuit simulators (LTspice, Hspice etc.), students will model, simulate, and analyze specific circuit architectures tailored to their research focus. Depending on student interest, design modules can be implemented using different types of TFTs, such as Organic TFT (OTFT) or Metal Oxide TFT technologies. Eligible design modules include digital logic blocks (such as inverters and ring oscillators), memory elements (such as SRAM or dynamic gain cells), or basic analog and digital (combinational and sequential) circuits. The simulation framework leverages specialized thin-film semiconductor compact models to accurately capture material-specific non-idealities, including low or disparate charge carrier mobilities, high threshold voltages, contact resistance, and bias stress instabilities. The core of this research involves evaluating critical circuit performance metrics, such as propagation delay, power dissipation, voltage transfer characteristics, or noise margins (e.g., Static Noise Margin for memory designs). Furthermore, the study explores optimization strategies—including geometric scaling, transistor sizing, and supply voltage tuning—to mitigate the inherent limitations of thin-film materials and parameter variations. By correlating individual thin-film device physics with macro-level circuit performance, this work establishes practical guidelines for designing robust integrated circuits. Ultimately, this work bridges the gap between thin-film semiconductor physics and practical EDA (Electronic Design Automation) tool applications, providing students with a flexible yet rigorous entry point into next-generation electronics research.
keywords: Thin-Film Transistors (TFTs), Circuit Simulation (EDA), Performance Optimization, Integrated Circuits (ICs)

title: Application and incorporation of vanadium dioxide for field effect transistors
description: Vanadium dioxide having its unique phase transition characteristics and insulator to metal transition characteristics, it can bring in interesting breakthroughs for designing new generation of field effect transistors.
keywords: VO2; IMT; gate oxide; FET; transition ratio

title: Implementation of periodic insulator to metal transition of vanadium dioxide for gas and analyte sensing
description: Fast switching of VO2 IMT transition can reduce the use of pulsed lasers, and bring in innovative method of gas sensing and analyte sensing.
keywords: VO2; IMT; laser chopper

title: Incorporation of vanadium dioxide in surface acoustic wave generators
description: VO2 can be included as an integral part of design of new generation of surface acoustic wave (SAW) generator devices. The periodic surface acoustic wave signals can periodically induce IMT transition in the VO2 film.
keywords: VO2; SAW device; piezoresistivity

title: Swarm Intelligence-Based Energy Management for Heterogeneous Second-Life EV Battery Energy Storage Systems in PV-Coupled Microgrids
description: The surge in decommissioned electric vehicle (EV) energy storage units introduces a critical dual challenge regarding ecological footprint and resource recovery. Utilizing these assets in second-life battery (SLB) configurations provides a viable pathway to minimize expenses and resource depletion; however, their financial viability is strictly contingent upon real-time operational strategies. While the physical correlations linking depth-of-discharge (DoD) and charge–discharge intensity (C-rate) to electrochemical degradation are well-documented, the long-term economic studies under variable grid duty cycles lack comprehensive investigation. To address this gap, this study develops a degradation-aware, multi-objective optimization framework for integrating heterogeneous SLBs into a photovoltaic (PV)-coupled charging hub serving EVs.
keywords: Second-Life EV Batteries; Swarm Intelligence Optimization; Microgrid; Energy Management; Demand Response

title: An Epidemic-Branching Model for Predicting Cascading Overload Failures in the Dhaka Urban Distribution Network Under Climate-Driven Load Surges
description: The power distribution network of Dhaka, managed by the Power Grid Company of Bangladesh (PGCB) and local utilities (DESCO/DPDC), operates under extreme structural stress during the hot and humid monsoon months. Rising ambient temperatures trigger concurrent spikes in residential and commercial cooling loads, driving the grid to its absolute thermal thresholds. When the network is highly stressed, a single localized contingency—such as a transformer trip at a critical substation like Aminbazar or Rampura—forces instantaneous power rerouting. Traditional contingency analysis often struggles with the computational speed required to map the rapid, stochastic nature of these real-time failures. This research treats cascading grid overloads not merely as isolated electrical events, but as a contagious physical phenomenon. By translating epidemiological network frameworks to electrical topologies, this project models structural outages as a propagating "disease vector" to predict and arrest total grid collapse in Dhaka.
keywords: Cascading Failures; Epidemic Network Theory; Dynamic Thermal Rating; Smart Grid Security; Urban Distribution Networks
